coredata.dc:title: Preparation of MDEA derived nitrogen-doped CQDs nanofluids and their CO2 capturing performance and mechanism investigation
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Wenjing
Zhang, Kefan
Xu, Chenghua
Zhang, Fan
Du, Lei
coredata.dc:description: Nanofluids acting as the third-generation absorbents are raising concern. However, the complex preparation method, high material cost, and instability hinder their further promotion. Herein, N-methyldiethanolamine (MDEA) as a classic organic amine solution was first used to prepare nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by a mild method, and the effective CQDs yield was determined by TEM, FT-IR, XPS, and fluorescence spectrometry. Generated nitrogen-doped CQDs have abundant surface function groups, a narrow size distribution (1–10 nm), and stable fluorescent properties. Moreover, CQDs nanofluids stability was comprehensively evaluated by Turbiscan stability analyzer, Zeta potential analyzer, and solid content analysis. The results indicated a high stability of CQDs in the amine solution. The CO2 capture performance of CQDs nanofluids was evaluated by a bubble reactor, and the maximum absorption enhancement index reached 1.27 in the mixed amine solution. The desorption process was also improved and nearly 6 % heat duty was saved. Finally, the enhancement mechanism of CQDs nanofluids was discussed. In contrast to other studies focusing on the mass transfer effect of nanofluids on macroscopic kinetics, the microscopic interaction effect between CQDs and CO2 was discussed by in-situ FT-IR and XPS. The results prove that apart from the physical adsorption effect, a chemical reaction also exists to capture CO2 by the nitrogen-doped structure and basic groups on CQDs surface, which endow the unique enhancement effect for CO2 capture system by CQDs. In general, the advantageous performance ensured the potential of CQDs nanofluids as advanced absorbents to achieve a low manufacturing cost and scale application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanofluids
CO2 capture
Carbon materials
In-situ FT-IR

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The microscopic characterization of CQDs nanofluids
The suspension stability of CQDs nanofluids
Stability and optical property of CQDs nanofluids
CO2 capture performance enhancement of CQDs nanofluids
Enhancement mechanism of CO2 absorption in CQDs nanofluids

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Research Foundation of Chengdu University of Information Technology
Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province ( 2022NSFSC1238 ) and Research Foundation of Chengdu University of Information Technology ( KYTZ202117 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The real photo and schematic diagram of CO2 absorption and desorption system.
(a) TEM image of CQDs, (b) the size distribution of CQDs, (c) HRTEM image of CQDs, (d) XPS spectra of CQDs, (e) FT-IR spectra of MDEA and CQDs and (f) the real photo of CQDs nanofluids.

(a) Delta backscattering light of CQDs nanofluids (5 %CQDs + 30 %MEA water solution) within 1 h, (b) the absolute Zeta potential of CQDs nanofluids with different base fluids, and (c) the time dependence of solid content in CQDs slurry, CQDs nanofluids, and CQDs nanofluids after used in CO2 absorption test.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of 1% CQDs-water and 30% MEA water solution. The inset shows a photograph of CQDs solution irradiated by the laser. (b) pH effect on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs nanofluid.
(a) The output amount profile of CO2 and (b) CO2 absorption improvement ratio in 30% MEA solution with different concentrations of CQDs.
(a) The CO2 absorption improvement ratio in 30% MEA solution with different concentrations of CQDs under different temperatures and (b) in different base fluids.

FT-IR spectra of the 10 %CQDs nanofluids before and after CO2 absorption (a), FT-IR spectra of CQDs powder exposure to CO2 within 60 min (b) and 30 min (c), high resolution N1s spectra (d), O1s spectra (e) and C1s spectra (f) of the CQDs before (Top) and after (Below) CO2 absorption.
The enhancement mechanism of CQDs for CO2 absorption.
The CO2 absorption enhancement of different nanofluids in the bubble reactor.
Desorption rate, cyclic capacity, and heat duty for 30% MEA solution and CQDs nanofluids with regeneration temperature at 90 °C.

head.ce:title: Preparation of MDEA derived nitrogen-doped CQDs nanofluids and their CO2 capturing performance and mechanism investigation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Resources and Environment, Chengdu University of Information Technology, Chengdu 610225, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Resources and Environment, Chengdu University of Information Technology, Chengdu 610225, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Thermo-Fluid Science and Engineering of MOE, School of Energy and Power Engineering, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Thermo-Fluid Science and Engineering of MOE
School of Energy and Power Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Thermo-Fluid Science and Engineering of MOE, School of Energy and Power Engineering, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanofluids acting as the third-generation absorbents are raising concern. However, the complex preparation method, high material cost, and instability hinder their further promotion. Herein, N-methyldiethanolamine (MDEA) as a classic organic amine solution was first used to prepare nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by a mild method, and the effective CQDs yield was determined by TEM, FT-IR, XPS, and fluorescence spectrometry. Generated nitrogen-doped CQDs have abundant surface function groups, a narrow size distribution (1–10 nm), and stable fluorescent properties. Moreover, CQDs nanofluids stability was comprehensively evaluated by Turbiscan stability analyzer, Zeta potential analyzer, and solid content analysis. The results indicated a high stability of CQDs in the amine solution. The CO2 capture performance of CQDs nanofluids was evaluated by a bubble reactor, and the maximum absorption enhancement index reached 1.27 in the mixed amine solution. The desorption process was also improved and nearly 6 % heat duty was saved. Finally, the enhancement mechanism of CQDs nanofluids was discussed. In contrast to other studies focusing on the mass transfer effect of nanofluids on macroscopic kinetics, the microscopic interaction effect between CQDs and CO2 was discussed by in-situ FT-IR and XPS. The results prove that apart from the physical adsorption effect, a chemical reaction also exists to capture CO2 by the nitrogen-doped structure and basic groups on CQDs surface, which endow the unique enhancement effect for CO2 capture system by CQDs. In general, the advantageous performance ensured the potential of CQDs nanofluids as advanced absorbents to achieve a low manufacturing cost and scale application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para: The liquid level of sample, μm
The average absorption rate of CO2 without CQDs, mol/min
The average absorption rate of CO2 with CQDs, mol/min
The backscattering light intensity in ith
The volume fraction of inlet CO2, %
The volume fraction of outlet CO2, %
The volume flow rate of inlet gas, m3/min
The volume flow rate of outlet gas, m3/min
The given liquid high of sample, μm

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon capture is the key part of Carbon capture, Utilization and Storage (CCUS). The numerous approaches have been developed and can be summarized in three categories: oxyfuel combustion, pre-combustion, and post-combustion capture. Among them, post-combustion owns the most promising commercial prospects, because it is easily achieved by a downstream retrofit for existing plants. Many technologies have been developed for post-combustion carbon capture, including solvent-based absorption, adsorption, member separation and mineralization, in which the solvent-based absorption is the most mature post-combustion carbon capture method, and its history can be traced back to 1970s [1]. Amine absorbents are very popular in industrial processes, and currently, the large-scale CCUS facilities have been operating in Canada, USA, and Norway based on aqueous amine capture systems [2]. However, traditional amine absorbents such as aqueous monoethanolamine (MEA) suffer from a significant energy penalty during regeneration unit which could reduce nearly 19.5–40 % of the energy capacity of a power plant, limiting their further development [3]. Therefore, absorbents are the core for improving the comprehensive performance of the solvent-based absorption methods. For example, a higher CO2 absorption rate of absorbents can reduce the size of device and a lower react heat can save energy consumption.

To exploit the high performance of CO2 capture, many advanced absorbents have been developed, such as blend amine solutions, water-lean absorbents, and phase separation absorbents. Recently, a new strategy has been considered to enhance the mass and heat transfer during the absorption–desorption process by introducing a third disperse phase such as nanoparticles [4]. Nanofluids as uniform solid suspensions with excellent heat transfer properties are widely employed in heat exchange systems [5]. Considering the analogy between heat and mass transfer, nanofluids are expected to enhance mass transfer. Krishnamurthy et al. proved this by visualizing dye diffusion in nanofluids at a higher rate [6]. The CO2 absorption improvement has also been widely determined with different types of nanofluids, and considering the cost of nanoparticles, oxide-based nanofluids are more likely to be popularized [7]. For example, Arshadi et al. used amino-modified Fe3O4 as an additive for CO2 absorption in a bubble column reactor, and the highest enhancement of CO2 absorption reached 70.3 % [8]. Lee et al. found Al2O3 and SiO2 nanoparticles can increase 9 % and 8 % of CO2 absorption by methanol in the tray column, and they also found a remarkable improvement of regeneration rate of nanofluids with 16 % and 20 % increases, respectively [9]. They also supported that the bubble breaking, the shuttle effect, and the hydrodynamic effect could explain the enhancement of CO2 absorption rate. However, the discussion about the micro-scale interaction between metal oxide solids and CO2 is easily overlooked because most nanoparticles lack surface functional groups. Moreover, with further research, metal oxide based nanofluids display some application defects, including corrosion [10], pH sensitivity [11], and increasing fluid viscosity [12].

Carbon materials with high thermal conductivity are usually studied in heat transfer fields. In contrast, few studies have focused on the mass transfer phenomenon in carbon based nanofluids. However, based on exited research results, we found that carbon materials generally displayed better enhancement performance for CO2 absorption than metal oxide nanoparticles. For example, Rahmatmand et al. compared the CO2 absorption performance with different types of nanofluids and carbon nanotubes (CNTs), which displayed the greatest impact on CO2 absorption capacity with an enhancement rate of 23 % with respect to pure MDEA solution [13]. Nabipour et al. also found a higher enhancement of CO2 absorption performance in Sulfinol-M solution with MWCNTs nanofluids (23.2 %) than Fe3O4 nanofluids (14.7 %) [14]. However, carbon materials are commonly hardly dispersed and insolubilized, so uniform dispersion becomes the main obstacle for their study in mass transfer. Rahimi et al. discussed the effect of hydrophobic/hydrophilic MWCNTs nanofluids on CO2 absorption and found that compared with acid-treated hydrophilic MWCNTs, amino-modified hydrophobic MWCNTs nanofluids displayed the highest improvement capacity [15]. However, the amino modified hydrophobic MWCNTs nanofluids were only stable in base solution. During CO2 absorption process, the pH change is inevitable, and the performance stability of carbon based nanofluids has to stand its effect. Therefore, finding a carbon based nanofluids with amino-groups and high stability in pH changed processing is meaningful. With the rapid development of nanotechnology, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as an upstart in the carbon material family brings new hope to reach the targe.

CQDs were discovered in 2004 during CNTs purification and were commonly quasi-spherical particles with diameters between 3 and 20 nm. As a promising nanomaterial, CQDs possess several advantages, including low cytotoxicity, easy fabrication, and good dispersity in polar solution, and can be widely used in biosensors [16], biomedicine [17], catalysts [18,19] and so on. CQDs as novel nanofluids were first discussed as heat transfer fluids in 2017 [20–22]. Apart from the remarkable increase in thermal conductivity, 49.97 % reduction in viscosity of base fluids with CQDs, which is distinguished from other nanofluids, avoids some engineering problems caused by the viscosity increasing with particle addition [21]. Moreover, CQDs nanofluids displayed ultra-stability under different work temperatures and heat cycling conditions, which could be considered as solar absorbers in solar thermal systems [23,24]. Obviously, the advantages of CQDs nanofluids can support their application potential in CO2 absorption, but there is still a gap in the study on the mass transfer of CQDs nanofluids. In particular, CQDs commonly have a smaller size distribution than other nanoparticles, and their size effect on mass transfer enhancement during CO2 absorption should be discussed. In this study, we attempt to prepare nitrogen-doped CQDs from MDEA by the microwave method. MDEA was first used as a raw material to generate CQDs, which could generate CQDs with the size between < 10 nm and display ultra-high stability in amine solution. The CO2 absorption–desorption experiment was conducted in a bubble column to investigate the performance enhancement of CQDs with amine solution and the blend amine solution. Moreover, FT-IR, in-situ FT-IR and XPS were employed to discuss the enhancement mechanism of CQDs nanofluids in depth, and the results showed that apart from physisorption, chemisorption also occurred during the whole absorption process resulting in the superior CO2 capture performance of CQDs nanofluids.

Monoethanolamine (MEA, purity > 99 %), N-methyldiethanolamine (MDEA, purity > 99 %) were purchased from KeLong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China) as the main base fluids of CO2 absorbents. Carbon dioxide (CO2, purity > 99.95 %) and nitrogen (N2, purity > 99.95 %) were supported by Heping Gas company (Chengdu, China).

CQDs were synthesized by the microwave method modified from our previous work [25]. In brief, 100 mL of MDEA was added to a 250 mL beaker and microwave irradiated for 30 min at 700 W. The color of the solution converted from transparent to black meaning CQDs generated. After cooling to room temperature, the CQDs solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min, and the upper solution was filtered with 0.22 µm membrane to remove large particles. The obtained filtrate was then subjected to dialysis (1000 Da) in deionized water for 24 h to remove residual MDEA. Finally, CQDs were freeze-dried for 8 h, and the brown solid was obtained and weighed.

After determining the content of CQDs in the dialyzed solution, a certain volume of dialyzed CQDs slurry was added to the base fluids (MEA or MDEA-water solution) and mixed evenly by ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. Without adding any surfactant, CQDs based organic amine nanofluids were prepared.

Fig. 1 shows the schematic diagram of CO2 absorption and desorption experiment. This system comprises a gas supply system, a bubble absorption reaction vessel, and an online gas analysis system. Two mass flow controllers (MFCs) were used to control the mass flow of CO2 and N2 to regulate CO2 concentration in the mixture gas. The bubble absorption reaction vessel was made of plexiglass with a volume of 250 mL. The reaction vessel was immersed in a water bath to maintain a constant temperature of the absorbent. Then, N2 gas was introduced into the vessel to remove the ambient air. Finally, a certain volume concentration of CO2 mixed with N2 was introduced with a certain mass flow into the reaction vessel to start the absorption test, and the concentration of the outlet gas was measured by an online flue gas analyzer. The entire absorption test was conducted within 300 min. Each test was conducted in triplicate, and the average value was reported.

The following equations can be built based on the mass balance:

are the volume fractions of the inlet and outlet CO2, respectively. V is the volume of reactor (250 × 10−6 m3), P is the system pressure equaled to 101325 Pa, R is the ideal gas constant equaled to 8.314 J/(mol·K) and T is system temperature (K).

Moreover, to compare the absorption improvement of CQDs in CO2 absorption, the improvement ratio (IR ) was introduced as follows:
(5)

(mol/min) are the average absorption rates of CO2 in the absorbent when it reaches saturation with or without CQDs.

To verify the feasibility of Eq. (3), the reaction vessel without any absorbent was filled with N2, and then introduced 10 % CO2 mixture gas with the flow rate of 100 mL/min. Under this condition, the absorption rate of CO2 equaled zero, namely
R
CO 2
=
0. Therefore, the functional relationship between
y
CO 2
and t can be obtained as shown in Fig. S1, which agrees well with the experimental data.

The desorption test was conducted in the same system of CO2 absorption experiment. After absorption was completed, the reaction vessel was moved to a thermostatically controlled system to maintain the temperature at 50 °C. To ensure that the water bath temperature improved to 90 °C, the reaction vessel was placed back in the water bath. The mixture gas was then introduced into the reaction vessel at a flow rate of 100 mL/min, and the concentration of CO2 in the outlet gas was measured using an online flue gas analyzer. The desorption rate and desorption amount of CO2 can also be calculated using Eq. (3) and Eq. (4).

To ensure the variable of experimental results, all of the tests were repeated three times with errors of less than 5 %. Moreover, the uncertainty analysis of CO2 absorption and desorption test was performed as follows [26]:
(6)
U =
e
T

Q
2
where U is the experimental uncertainty and e is the measurement errors for each parameter. Specifically, eT represents the temperature measurement error with 0.8 %,
e
R
abs
and
e
R
des
represent the measurement error of CO2 absorption and desorption rate with 2.9 % and 3.6 %, respectively, and eQ represents the measurement error of gas flow rate with 1 %. Hence, the experimental uncertainty was about ± 4.8 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The microscopic characterization of CQDs nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para: The microscopic morphology of CQDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G220 microscope), and the image was processed by Digital Micrograph. The changes in chemical groups before and after reacting with CO2 were recorded by a Fourier Transformed Infrared spectrophotometer (FT-IR, Nicolet iS50, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) in different modes. The fluorescence of CQDs was measured by a spectrofluorimeter (HITACHI High-Technologies Corporation, Japan). The surface element characteristics of CQDs were measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientic K-Alpha, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The suspension stability of CQDs nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para: The suspension stability of CQDs nanofluids was evaluated comprehensively under different conditions. The qualitative evaluation was conducted based on the Zeta potential value by a Zeta potential measurer (Brookhaven Instruments Corporation, USA) and Turbiscan Stability Index (TSI) value by a Turbiscan stability analyzer (Formulation, France). Quantitative evaluation was conducted by measuring the solid content in the supernatant liquid based on the weight method.

The MDEA derived CQDs were characterized by TEM, FT-IR, and XPS. As TEM image depicted in Fig. 2 (a), CQDs are spherical and monodisperse particles with regular shapes. Based on the size distribution of CQDs, as shown in Fig. 2(b), the diameter of particles ranges from 1 to 10 nm with an average value of 3.84 nm. Fig. 2(c) shows the HRTEM image of CQDs, and the inset shows the result after image processing. CQDs display a single crystal structure with an interlayer spacing of 0.22 nm corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite. A similar value of interlayer spacing was observed in other works [24,27]. The element composition was further determined by XPS, and the result is shown in Fig. 2(d). Three peaks at 532.3, 399.5, and 285.1 eV correspond to the C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s signals, respectively. This indicates that CQDs consist mainly of C, O, and N, and their atomic ratios are 50.9 %, 38.3 %, and 10.8 %, respectively, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2(d).

Moreover, FT-IR analysis was conducted to determine the surface functional groups on CQDs. Fig. 2(e) shows the FT-IR spectra of MDEA and CQDs. An obvious change can be found in MDEA after treated by microwave irradiation. To be specific, the peaks located in the wavenumbers between 2700 and 3000 cm−1 disappear in the FT-IR curve of CQDs, which are attributed to the stretching vibration region of C-H bond. Correspondingly, the in-plane and out-plane flexural vibration peaks of C-H bonds (1475 to 650 cm−1) also disappear. This indicates that the carbon atom in MDEA as a carbon source has been used to generate CQDs. Conversely, some N related characteristic functional groups are still generated on CQDs. Compared with MDEA, a broad absorption peak appears at 3400 cm−1 covering the peak at 3550 cm−1, which could be due to the stretching vibration of N-H bonds generated by microwave causing a stronger absorption peak compared to that of O-H bonds [28]. The peaks at 1632, 1402 and 850 cm−1 are associated with amide I bands (C = N or C = O), C-N, and N-H stretching vibrations, respectively. A similar result was also reported in Stefanakis’ and Lis’ works, who both attempted to prepare CQDs from nitrogen-containing compounds, and similar amide functional groups were found on CQDs [29,30]. Moreover, oxygenic functional groups are also present on the surface of CQDs. The peak at 1102 cm−1 can be assigned to C-O bonds. In summary, after microwave irradiation, the hydrocarbon skeleton of MDEA is pyrolyzed, and the amide functional groups and oxygenic functional groups are generated on the surface of CQDs, which enhances the dispersibility and stability of CQDs in base fluids without adding any surfactant, and increases the absorption effect of CQDs nanofluids for CO2. Fig. 2(f) shows a real photo of CQDs nanofluids with different concentrations. CQDs are well dispersed in the base fluid and exhibit a uniform system, which ensures the subsequent application of CQDs nanofluids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and optical property of CQDs nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para:
As a suspension system, the stability of CQDs nanofluids is one of the most important properties to ensure their application performance in CO2 capture systems. During the CO2 absorption process, particle deposition is very dangerous for actual operation, which could cause the abnormal pressure drop and even block the pipeline. Hence, the stability as the application premise is comprehensively discussed. First, the optical change of suspension was scanned by a Turbiscan stability analyzer, in which fresh-prepared CQDs nanofluids (5 % CQDs + 30 % MEA water solution) were added to a sample tube, and the change in delta backscattering light (ΔBS) was recorded within 1 h. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), each color of the curves represents the scan results at different standing times, and the color changes from red to blue from 0 to 1 h. Apart from the obvious change on the top level of liquid at a height of 40–50 mm, which was caused by the interference of surface tension, the fluctuation of delta backscattering light was slight with an increase in time. Moreover, the value of TSI is calculated as Eq. (7) and the value of TSI is 0.234 lower than 0.5, further indicating the good stability of CQDs nanofluids.
(7)
TSI=
∑ i
∑ h
scan
i
h
-
scan
i - 1
( h )
H
( % )
where H is the liquid level of sample,
scan
i
h
a n d
scan
i - 1
h
is the backscattering light intensity in ith and (i-1)th scan at given liquid high (h), respectively.

Moreover, the Zeta potential values of 5 % CQDs with different base fluids were measured, and their absolute values are shown in Fig. 3(b). The change in Zeta potential value is not significant and all values are over 42 mV, which suggests that CQDs nanofluids have good stability in water, and different concentrations or components of organic amine solution. The long-term stability of CQDs nanofluids was also evaluated. Fig. 3(c) shows the time dependence of solid content in the upper solution for CQDs slurry (in water), fresh CQDs nanofluids, and CQDs nanofluids after used in CO2 absorption test. The solid content of CQDs slurry changes obviously decreasing from 1070 to 1022 mg/L. This can be explained by the high concentration of CQDs, which compresses the double electrode layer and reduce the electrostatic repulsion between particles, causing the formation of agglomeration and precipitation [31]. However, the solid contents of fresh and after used CQDs nanofluids (5 %CQDs + 30 %MEA water solution) change slightly with increase of time. This indicates that CQDs nanofluids with lower CQDs content (∼55 mg/L) have better stability within 48 h. The suspension stability of CQDs nanofluids can still be maintained after suffering the CO2 absorption process, which reflects that the change in pH and temperature during the absorption reaction will not affect the stability of CQDs nanofluids [24].

Unlike other carbon-based materials, CQDs possess unique photoluminescence (PL) properties, and the size of the π-conjugate domain determines their PL behavior [30]. Namely, with an increase in CQDs size, the band-gap energy will decrease and the PL spectra will undergo a red shift until loss of the PL properties. Hence, the change of PL properties in CQDs nanofluid could reflect the size change in CQDs in the base fluid. The PL of CQDs was characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 4 (a). Under excitation at 380 nm, a strong fluorescence emission peak can be found around 455 nm for the 1 % CQDs water solution. In contrast, no fluorescence emission peak appears for 30 % MEA water solution at the same wavelength range. In Fig. 4(a), the inset photograph displays when a laser passes through the samples and an obvious luminous beam can be observed in CQDs solution rather than in 30 % MEA water solution. Moreover, considering the pH change during CO2 absorption in organic amine solution, the pH effect on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs nanofluids was discussed, and the results are shown in Fig. 4(b). In the acid condition, pH effect is significant and the fluorescence intensity of CQDs nanofluids decreases by nearly half as the pH decreases from 5 to 1. In the alkaline condition, the pH effect is not as strong as in the acid condition, and the fluorescence intensity of CQDs nanofluids can keep over 600 at pH 11. The dependence of fluorescence properties on pH reflects that some oxygen-containing functional groups exist on the surface of CQDs, which are easily to be protonated under acidic pH, breaking the electrostatic balance of particles and further leading to particles aggregation. As a result, the fluorescence intensity decreased. However, during the amine solution-based CO2 absorption process, the solution pH will change between 7 and 12 [32]. Therefore, CQDs nanofluids can remain suspension stable and well dispersion with an effective particle size range to meet the application scenarios of CO2 capture systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CO2 capture performance enhancement of CQDs nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para: The CO2 absorption enhancement test was first conducted using a 30 % MEA solution, and this concentration has been used for CO2 capture in a large-scale. The output amount of CO2 was detected as a function of time for pure 30 % MEA solution and solutions with 5 %-10 % CQDs at 25 °C, and the results are presented in Fig. 5 (a). The whole absorption processes of MEA solution with or without CQDs are similar, and can be divided into three stages. In the first stage, the output amount of CO2 for CQDs nanofluids is obviously lower than that of CO2 for pure MEA solution before 20 min, and the output amount of CO2 decreases with increasing concentration of CQDs in MEA solution from 2.91 % to 1.24 % at 60 min. After 60 min, the CO2 absorption process reaches the second stage, in which the absorption rates of nanofluids gradually slow down due to the early consumption of MEA solution and reach the same output amount of CO2 between the pure MEA solution and nanofluids at 95 min. In this stage, the mass transfer process gradually transitions from diffusion-controlled to kinetic-controlled, so the enhancement of CQDs for CO2 absorption process is hidden. In the last stage, the absorption rates of both systems decrease, and absorption saturation is reached faster in nanofluids with higher CQDs loads. Similar absorption curves of CO2 were also observed in other studies [33,34]. Considering their suggestions, the absorption performance enhancement of CQDs nanofluids for CO2 was discussed in 60 min, and the CO2 absorption improvement ratio was calculated by Eq. (5), as shown Fig. 5 (b). It is clear that the improvement ratio increases with increasing concentration of CQDs, and the maximum value is 1.12 (∼12 %) when CQDs concentration reaches 10 %. Compared with the results in other studies, this value is significant in the MEA solution. For example, in Jiang’s work, the maximum improvement ratio was 1.09 with 600 mg/L TiO2 [35]. It could be that CQDs with smaller sizes have a better effect on mass diffusion. Moreover, it is worth mentioning that no obvious inflection point occurs, as described in previous studies with increasing concentrations of nanoparticles [34,35]. This could be because in this study, the solid loading of 10 % CQDs nanofluids is about 110 mg/L, which is far lower than that in other studies. Hence, the particle aggregation and viscosity increase causing weaken in improvement effect did not occur. These findings indicate that CQDs have a cost advantage over other materials to improve the CO2 absorption process.

Fig. 6 (a) shows the temperature effect on CO2 absorption improvement ratio at different concentrations of CQDs nanofluids. In general, with increasing temperature from 20 to 40 °C, the improvement ratios decrease for all CQDs nanofluids. This indicates that MEA solution is the main body for CO2 absorption process, in which temperature has a negative effect. However, the degree of temperature effect is different for different samples, which is obviously stronger for lower concentration samples based on the color scale change in Fig. 6 (a). This could imply that CQDs have a positive effect on the whole CO2 absorption process, and the higher loading of CQDs displays a stronger positive effect, so for 10 % CQDs nanofluids, the improvement ratios only decrease from 1.12 to 1.01 with temperature increase. However, this tendency is inverse in a reported study, and they suggested that particle aggregation would weaken CO2 absorption performance with temperature increase in high solid loading [33]. In fact, CQDs nanofluids have been reported to have high thermal stability as heat transfer fluids, so in this study, the temperature effect on the base fluid plays the main role, and the improvement effect of CQDs is not failure [23,36].

Considering the high energy consumption of MEA regeneration, mixing MDEA or other amines in MEA solution as a novel blended amine absorbent is one of the strategies. Herein, the improvement ratios of CO2 absorption in CQDs nanofluids with different base fluids are also discussed and the results are shown in Fig. 6 (b). The improvement ratios for all CQDs nanofluids in MDEA solution are the lowest. This indicates that CQDs mainly improve the diffusion control process, but CO2 absorption in MDEA is a chemical control process [37]. Therefore, the improvement of CO2 absorption by CQDs nanofluids in MDEA base fluid is limited, but there still exists an improvement trend with increasing concentration of CQDs. Moreover, the improvement ratios of CO2 absorption in blended amines are all higher than that in pure amine solution, and the highest value can reach 1.27 (10 % CQDs) in 10 % MEA + 20 % MDEA solution. It could be that the drop of CO2 absorption rate in blends amine is smoother and the diffusion control process is prolonged with MDEA addition, so the improvement effect of CQDs is stronger.

Moreover, the CO2 absorption enhancement of different nanofluids in the bubble reactor are summarized in Table 1 . Unfortunately, the reported research on CO2 absorption in carbon material based nanofluids is lacking in the bubble reactor. Conversely, considering the pore structure and high specific surface area, more studies have focused on the absorption capacity enhancement by carbon materials. For example, the absorption capacity of CO2 can be enhanced by approximately 9.1 %, 31 % and 26.4 % for base fluids by graphene oxide, MWCNTs, NH2-MWCNTs nanofluids in the batch reactor [15,38,39]. However, for the bubble reactor, the absorption rate enhancement is more significant because nanoparticles can also increase the air–liquid contact area, except for enhancing the diffusion effect in base fluids. Therefore, the improvement ratios of CO2 absorption in amine solutions with CQDs are competitive with those of other metal oxides, as shown in Table 1, especially for blended amines. Moreover, the CO2 loading in 10 % CQDs nanofluids was measured at 0.46 mol/mol and that in pure MEA solution was 0.41 mol/mol. Nearly 12 % enhancement can be achieved for CO2 absorption capacity by CQDs nanofluids in absorption equilibrium. This result suggests that the enhancement of the absorption rate and capacity for CO2 occurred in CQDs nanofluids.

The addition of CQDs in the amine absorbent is also expected to lead to different desorption performance of CO2. To determine the effect of CQDs for the regeneration performance of the CO2 absorbent, a desorption test was conducted at 90 °C, and the results are shown in Fig. S2. Clearly, the CO2 loading of 30 % MEA solution and CQDs nanofluids dropped sharply at first 120 min and then turned to gentle from 120 to 300 min due to the decrease of the driving force of CO2 desorption with the decrease of CO2 loading. Therefore, the following analysis was conducted based on the data at the first 120 min to show the effect of CQDs. The desorption rate of absorbent was obtained from the curve in Fig. S2, in which the slope of the first 60 min was defined as the initial desorption rate, Rd (mol-CO2/mol-absorbent/min). The desorption amount of CO2 within 120 min was defined as the cyclic capacity (CC, mol/mol) of the absorbent, and its heat duty (kJ/mol-CO2) was calculated based on Eq. (8) [44].
(8)
Heatduty =
Heatinput / t i m e
Amount
CO
2
/ t i m e
=
Electricity ( k J )
CC × c ×
V a
( m o l )
where electricity was measured by an electric energy meter during the desorption test, and c (mol/L) and Va (L) are the concentration and volume of the absorbents, respectively.

Table 2 shows the desorption rate, cyclic capacity, and heat duty of 30 % MEA solution and CQDs nanofluids. CQDs nanofluids have a higher desorption rate and cyclic capacity and lower heat duty than pure MEA solution. This could be attributed to the enhancement of the activation effect, thermal effect, and surface effects by nanoparticles, as reported by Lee [9].

In general, the performance of absorbent can be improved by CQDs both in the absorption and regeneration processes. Hence, economic costs such as energy consumption and system scale are expected to be reduced by CQDs nanofluids in the carbon capture process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement mechanism of CO2 absorption in CQDs nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para: The enhancement mechanism of nanofluids during CO2 absorption has been widely studied and various mechanisms explain the transport and diffusion of CO2 in nanofluids, including the grazing effect, hydrodynamic effect, and bubble breaking effect [45]. For the bubble reactor, these mechanisms may enhance CO2 absorption in nanofluids, and these effects are all based on the interaction between CQDs and CO2. Therefore, to understand why the enhancement effect of CQDs nanofluids was better than that of other nanoparticles (for example Al2O3), we employed a series of characterizations to determine the unique advantages of MDEA derived CQDs nanofluids from their abundant nitrogenous groups.

First, the FT-IR spectra of CQDs before and after CO2 absorption were measured, as shown in Fig. 7 (a). There are two new peaks in the FT-IR spectra of CQDs-CO2, indicating the interaction effect on the surface of CQDs with CO2 during the absorption process. Among them, the peak at 1462 cm−1 may be attributed to the stretching vibration of C = O from –HN-C = O, which could be generated from the reaction between CO2 and –NH2 groups on CQDs (CQDs-NH2 + CO2 → CQDs-NHCOOH) [46]. The peak at 1383 cm−1 corresponds to the activated symmetric stretching mode of CO2, which indicates that the linearity of CO2 molecular has changed to carbonate due to the cation-induced dipole interactions from the CQDs surface [47].

However, the ex-situ FT-IR spectra only provide limited information about the change of groups on CQDs surface, especially for the low transmittance particles. Hence, the in-situ FT-IR was employed to display the whole adsorption process for CO2 on CQDs surface. The in-situ FT-IR spectra of CO2 adsorption on CQDs from 0 to 60 min were recorded, as shown in Fig. 7(b). There is a remarkable change in the intensity of the main peak at 2346 cm−1, which sequentially increases at first 30 min, as shown in Fig. 7(c). This peak is attributed to the asymmetric stretching mode of CO2, reflecting the physisorption on CQDs. Interestingly, the intensity of this peak decreases gradually as time increases from 30 to 60 min, that is, the physisorption of CO2 on CQDs weakened with the extension of adsorption time. Meanwhile, some other minor and broad peaks were wakened at about 1200–2000 and 3000–3500 cm−1, which could be attributed to the chemisorption of CO2 on CQDs. The band at 1625 cm−1 attributed to carboxylate species, indicating the generation of C = O on CQDs with adsorption time increased [47]. Moreover, a broad negative peak happened around 3000–3500 cm−1 reflecting the change of N-H bond, which could be explained by the decrease in the basic site on CQDs due to the interaction between CO2 and –NH2 groups [48]. In summary, the in-situ FT-IR analysis intuitively described the physisorption and chemisorption of CO2 on CQDs and the change of basic sites, and the generation of carboxylate species were also monitored. However, it is difficult to distinguish the different contributions of C-N, C = O, and N-H bonds at 1200–2000 cm−1 because of their overlap. Therefore, the identification of generated species on the basic sites of CQDs after adsorbed CO2 is roughly based on limited FT-IR data.

XPS analysis was conducted to distinguish the species on CQDs after the CO2 absorption process. In Fig. 7(d), the high-resolution XPS spectrum of N 1 s can be deconvoluted into four peaks for raw CQDs: graphitic N (∼401.3 eV), pyrrolic N (∼400.2 eV), pyridonic N/amine group (∼399.5 eV) and pyridinic N (∼398.3 eV), whose contents are 26.33 %, 10.43 %, 23.72 % and 39.52 %, respectively [49]. This indicates that except for the amino group on CQDs surface, the N also dopes in the carbon skeleton of CQDs. After absorbing CO2, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of N 1 s shows a marked change. After deconvoluted, the content of pyrrolic N decreases to 5.72 %, reflecting the reaction between N-H bond and CO2 on carbon skeleton (C-(NH)-C + CO2 → C-NCOOH-C). Similarly, graphitic N (C-(NC)-C) also could present some N-H bonds on the skeleton structure edge of CQDs, which could react with CO2 [50]. As a result, the content of graphitic N decreases to 20.60 %. On the other hand, the content of components at 399.5 eV increase to 33.47 %. This indicates that the carbonyl group is generated and bound on graphitic N and pyridinic N (O = CNC) [51]. Fig. 7(e) shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1 s. The divided peaks at 533.2, 532.4, and 531.6 eV are designated as O-H, C-O, and C = O bonds [17]. It is obvious that after CO2 absorption, the content of C = O increases from 34.21 % to 56.19 %, indicating the generation of carbonyl groups on CQDs again. Moreover, for the C 1 s XPS spectra shown in Fig. 7(f), the C-C bond peak is centered at 284.6 eV, corresponding to the carbon atom on the graphitic skeleton [49]. A long tailing peak in higher binding energy presents N or O atoms bonding to the C skeleton, and it is different in chemical bond distribution for CQDs before and after use for CO2 absorption. Among them, the content of C-N-C (284.62 eV) increases remarkably from 15.59 % to 40.50 % after absorption CO2, which could be attributed the reaction of amino group and CO2 (C-NH2 + CO2 → C-NHCOOH), and correspondingly, the peak of C-N bond (285.33 eV) of raw CQDs disappears. Moreover, corresponding to the O 1 s spectra, the binding state of C = O could be fitted at 288.69 eV after absorption CO2.

In conclusion, the whole enhancement mechanism of CQDs nanofluids for CO2 absorption is described in Scheme 1 . First, CQDs derived from MDEA by microwave heating generate the abundant amino and hydroxyl groups on their surface and further endow them with high dispersity and stability in base fluids. Hence, CQDs nanofluids can display remarkable mass transfer enhancement for gas–liquid absorption based on the grazing effect, hydrodynamic effect, and bubble breaking effect. Moreover, from the viewpoint of gas–solid interaction, the physisorption plays the main effect for CQDs capturing CO2 in base fluids. This result is in agreement with common oxide nanoparticles. However, when the adsorption equilibrium reaches, the chemical adsorption gradually plays its role, where the nitrogen-doped carbon skeleton and amino groups from CQDs also bring the rich active sites to react with CO2 and further enhance the absorption ability of CQDs nanofluids. Overall, compared with other materials, this study proposes an easy preparation, low cost, and high stability nanofluids, which displays considerable potential for application in CO2 capture.

The use of nanomaterials to enhance the CO2 absorption process has shown great potential in carbon capture technology, but it has to face some barriers on practical application, such as the high cost of nanomaterials, complex preparation process, and low suspension stability. Hence, in this study, CQDs nanofluids were proposed as a potential CO2 absorbent for fabrication from organic amines. Nitrogen-doped CQDs were first prepared from MDEA with a size distribution of 1–10 nm, and an abundance of basic groups on the surface of CQDs was determined by FT-IR. Moreover, MDEA generated CQDs also showed a strong fluorescence effect and could be maintained stable in the basic solution to ensure stable performance in the CO2 absorption process. The absorption performance of CO2 both displayed remarkable improvement in CQDs nanofluids, in which the maximum enhancement index reached 1.27 for mixture amine based CQDs nanofluids. The enhancement mechanism could be attributed to the physical adsorption and chemical adsorption effects, and the chemical reaction could occur between the amino groups of CQDs and CO2. Hence, in the future studies, the surface modification of nanoparticles might be the next study focus for nanofluids application, which combines the advantage of mass transfer from nanofluids and chemical adsorption from particle to enhance the CO2 capture process. In general, this study proposes a new strategy for nanofluids preparation to achieve their stable and high-efficient application on the spot for CO2 capture systems.

Wenjing Chen: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft. Kefan Zhang: Investigation, Methodology. Chenghua Xu: Resources, Supervision. Fan Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Lei Du: Formal analysis, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province (2022NSFSC1238) and Research Foundation of Chengdu University of Information Technology (KYTZ202117).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2023.126049.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
